ontology.foodex2_description: Chickpea / Bengal gram flour (besan), dry, milled from split chana dal
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chickpea flour as a food ingredient in current release; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chickpea flour used for batter (e.g., pakora) is approximately 30g, providing around 116 kcal, 6.7g protein, and 17.3g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chickpea Flour / Besan — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05040004; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database food code F05040004 and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for chickpea flour (besan). Both sources are on the approved list. Values are in close agreement.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — Chickpea flour / besan-based preparations; GI value ~39 derived from cooked besan-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: The GI of 39 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked besan-based dishes (e.g., besan roti, chickpea flour flatbread). Dry raw besan flour does not have a directly measurable GI as it is not consumed in raw form; the GI value applies to typical cooked preparations. Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as: (39 × 17.3g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.7, rounded to 6.8 consistent with source. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (846mg/100g) and magnesium (166mg/100g) with modest sodium (64mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Chickpea flour is a favourable choice for individuals with hypertension due to its high potassium-to-sodium ratio; potassium (846mg/100g) and magnesium (166mg/100g) are clinically associated with blood pressure reduction, while sodium content (64mg/100g) is modest and well within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~39), high protein (22.4g/100g), and high dietary fibre (10.8g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chickpea flour has one of the lowest GI values of any flour (~39), combined with 22.4g protein and 10.8g dietary fibre per 100g, making it an exceptional flour choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it substantially attenuates postprandial glucose excursions compared to wheat or rice flours.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.7g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (10.8g/100g), and high potassium (846mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chickpea flour is cardioprotective: it is very low in saturated fat (0.7g/100g), contains zero cholesterol, and provides 10.8g dietary fibre per 100g which supports LDL cholesterol reduction; its high potassium and magnesium content further supports vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high folate (437mcg DFE/100g), iron (6.2mg/100g), and magnesium (166mg/100g); gluten-free
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chickpea flour is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its very high folate content (437mcg DFE/100g) supports immune cell regulation, and it is naturally gluten-free with low anti-nutritional factors when cooked.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chickpea flour (besan or gram flour) is a fine, pale yellow flour milled from split Bengal gram (chana dal), a desi variety of chickpea (Cicer arietinum) with smaller, darker seeds than kabuli chickpeas. It has a slightly earthy, nutty flavour and a smooth, powdery texture. It is completely gluten-free and is one of the most nutritionally dense flours available: 22.4g protein, 10.8g dietary fibre, and only 57.8g carbohydrate per 100g, compared to approximately 10g protein and 75g carbohydrate in refined wheat flour. The glycemic index when cooked is very low (~39), making it the preferred flour for diabetic-friendly Indian cooking. It is extraordinarily high in folate (437mcg DFE/100g), essential for pregnant women and those at risk of folate deficiency. Rich in potassium (846mg/100g), magnesium (166mg/100g), iron (6.2mg/100g), phosphorus (318mg/100g), and zinc (2.8mg/100g). Used extensively across Indian, Pakistani, and Middle Eastern cuisines: as batter for pakora and bhajia, as the base for dhokla (steamed fermented cake), besan cheela (savoury pancakes), besan halwa (sweet), murukku (fried snack), and as a natural gluten-free thickener for gravies and curries. Also used in gluten-free baking as a wheat flour substitute.
common_user_queries: chickpea flour vs wheat flour nutrition
is gram flour good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F05040004) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, both approved primary sources. GI value referenced from University of Sydney GI Database for cooked besan preparations. All values are internally consistent and plausible for raw chickpea flour.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for chickpea flour is not reported in the HPB Foodo database (F05040004) or ICMR-NIN tables at this level of granularity. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food code. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — chickpea flour is a plant-derived food containing no cholesterol). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB/ICMR-NIN). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB/ICMR-NIN for raw dry flour). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-derived food). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived food contains no B12). GI value of 39 applies to cooked besan-based preparations; dry raw besan does not have a directly measurable GI. Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as (39 × 17.34g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 6.8. This is an Indian-origin ingredient widely used in Singapore and South Asian diaspora communities; multilingual names provided in Hindi, Malay, and Tamil. Chinese-script name not included as chickpea flour is not a traditional Chinese ingredient and no authoritative Chinese-language food composition entry was identified.
